subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 22, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Mortality and adverse events of extracorporeal haemoadsorption
sentences: Story 1 is about Mortality and adverse events of extracorporeal haemoadsorption therapy in cardiac surgery: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials..
This is a systematic review / meta-analysis from British journal of anaesthesia.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in adult patients undergoing cardiac surgery with the use of CPB, how should we interpret ay (mean difference of the intervention vs control group (pooled mean difference, 95% CI=-0.36, -1.31-0.60, P=0.42) when compared with standard CPB. However, confidence intervals included the possibility of clinically important benefit or harm. There was?
We included 12 RCTs with 713 participants.

Meta-analysis showed no statistically significant benefit of HA during CPB on mortality (odds in the intervention vs control group (odds ratio, 95% confidence interval [CI]=1.08, 0.59-2.01, P=0.75), hospital length of stay (mean difference of the intervention vs control group (pooled mean difference, 95% CI=0.21, -1.89-2.30, P=0.82) and intensive care length of stay (mean difference of the intervention vs control group (pooled mean difference, 95% CI=-0.36, -1.31-0.60, P=0.42) when compared with standard CPB.
However, confidence intervals included the possibility of clinically important benefit or harm.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.

The bedside implication is this: Potentially practice-relevant because a patient-centered outcome is reported; check absolute risk difference, adverse events, and whether your ICU population matches the study.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Facilitating Communication for Family Members of Critically Il
sentences: Story 2 is about Facilitating Communication for Family Members of Critically Ill Patients During and Following a Hospital Stay: A Randomized Controlled Trial..
This is a randomized / clinical trial from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
The difference in mean HADS-depression between intervention and control over 6 mo was not significantly different: -0.07 (95% CI, -0.70 to 0.55).
The primary outcome was family member depression, measured using the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) at four time points (randomization, 1-, 3-, and 6-mo postrandomization).

Among respondents, HADS-depression tended to decrease over time (mean ± sd = 7.2 ± 4.6 for control and 7.2 ± 4.2 for intervention at baseline; 5.9 ± 4.1 for control and 5.9 ± 4.5 for intervention at 6 mo).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Psychometric Properties of the Patient Dignity Inventory in a
sentences: Story 3 is about Psychometric Properties of the Patient Dignity Inventory in a Longitudinal Cohort of Acute Respiratory Failure Survivors..
This is a cohort study from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in adults in four University of Pennsylvania hospitals' ICUs, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?

We calculated MCID by averaging anchor-based (EuroQol-5 Dimension MCID = 0.11 from baseline to 12 months) and distribution-based (average of se of the mean, 0.3 × sd, and 5% of baseline score range) estimates.Of 967 eligible patients, 103 patients (11%) enrolled at baseline, with 70% retention at 12 months.
Seventy-five percent of score variance was attributable to patient differences and 25% to timepoint, yielding phi = 0.93 (excellent consistency).
Patients completed the PDI within 1 month of ICU discharge and at 3, 6, and 12 months.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as background evidence; look for confounding and whether the exposure is modifiable at the bedside.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Mortality and adverse events of extracorporeal haemoadsorption therapy in cardiac surgery: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials.
articles.summary: We included 12 RCTs with 713 participants. Meta-analysis showed no statistically significant benefit of HA during CPB on mortality (odds in the intervention vs control group (odds ratio, 95% confidence interval [CI]=1.08, 0.59-2.01, P=0.75), hospital length of stay (mean difference of the intervention vs control group (pooled mean difference, 95% CI=0.21, -1.89-2.30, P=0.82) and intensive care length of stay (mean difference of the intervention vs control group (pooled mean difference, 95% CI=-0.36, -1.31-0.60, P=0.42) when compared with standard CPB. However, confidence intervals included the possibility of clinically important benefit or harm. There was also no significant difference in adverse events or requirements for ventilation, cardiovascular support or renal replacement therapy. This meta-analysis did not show evidence of clinical benefit for routine use of HA during CPB for cardiac surgery, although clinically relevant benefit or harm cannot be excluded on the currently available evidence.
articles.analysis: Potentially practice-relevant because a patient-centered outcome is reported; check absolute risk difference, adverse events, and whether your ICU population matches the study.

keyResults: Meta-analysis showed no statistically significant benefit of HA during CPB on mortality (odds in the intervention vs control group (odds ratio, 95% confidence interval [CI]=1.08, 0.59-2.01, P=0.75), hospital length of stay (mean difference of the intervention vs control group (pooled mean difference, 95% CI=0.21, -1.89-2.30, P=0.82) and intensive care length of stay (mean difference of the intervention vs control group (pooled mean difference, 95% CI=-0.36, -1.31-0.60, P=0.42) when compared with standard CPB.
However, confidence intervals included the possibility of clinically important benefit or harm.
There was also no significant difference in adverse events or requirements for ventilation, cardiovascular support or renal replacement therapy.
criticalAppraisal: High-yield synthesis: check heterogeneity, included study quality, and whether ICU patients match your setting.
Look for heterogeneity, small-study effects, and whether pooled outcomes are clinically comparable.

articles.title: Facilitating Communication for Family Members of Critically Ill Patients During and Following a Hospital Stay: A Randomized Controlled Trial.
articles.summary: ICU patients (n = 449) with chronic life-limiting illness (2-yr median survival, and/or acute, severe illness with greater than 15% hospital mortality risk) and their family members (n = 505). The difference in mean HADS-depression between intervention and control over 6 mo was not significantly different: -0.07 (95% CI, -0.70 to 0.55). For family members of critically ill patients facing a high likelihood of death or serious disability, involving a communication facilitator during and after an ICU stay did not lead to a reduction in depressive symptoms. Families of patients admitted to ICUs experience numerous care transitions, poor communication across these transitions, and emotional distress associated, in part, with the challenges of obtaining care consistent with patients' values. Specialized support from ICU to postacute setting may improve outcomes.
articles.analysis: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.

pico.population: Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text.
pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: The primary outcome was family member depression, measured using the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) at four time points (randomization, 1-, 3-, and 6-mo postrandomization). Among respondents, HADS-depression tended to decrease over time (mean ± sd = 7.2 ± 4.6 for control and 7.2 ± 4.2 for intervention at baseline; 5.9 ± 4.1 for control and...

keyResults: The difference in mean HADS-depression between intervention and control over 6 mo was not significantly different: -0.07 (95% CI, -0.70 to 0.55).
The primary outcome was family member depression, measured using the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) at four time points (randomization, 1-, 3-, and 6-mo postrandomization).

Among respondents, HADS-depression tended to decrease over time (mean ± sd = 7.2 ± 4.6 for control and 7.2 ± 4.2 for intervention at baseline; 5.9 ± 4.1 for control and 5.9 ± 4.5 for intervention at 6 mo).

Differences in family-reported secondary outcomes were in the expected direction, including relationship with the healthcare team (p = 0.0162) and goal concordance (p = 0.0504), but their effect sizes were small.
criticalAppraisal: Interventional evidence: check allocation, blinding, protocol adherence, and absolute effect size.
Check randomization, concealment, blinding, missing data, and whether analysis was intention-to-treat.
articles.practiceImpact: Do not change practice on efficacy alone; focus journal-club discussion on whether the confidence intervals exclude a clinically important benefit or harm.

articles.title: Psychometric Properties of the Patient Dignity Inventory in a Longitudinal Cohort of Acute Respiratory Failure Survivors.
articles.summary: Although greater than 50% of acute respiratory failure (ARF) survivors develop postintensive care syndrome, dignity-centered interventions-shown to improve psychosocial and existential outcomes in other longitudinally impaired populations-remain largely unexplored in ARF survivorship. We calculated MCID by averaging anchor-based (EuroQol-5 Dimension MCID = 0.11 from baseline to 12 months) and distribution-based (average of se of the mean, 0.3 × sd, and 5% of baseline score range) estimates.Of 967 eligible patients, 103 patients (11%) enrolled at baseline, with 70% retention at 12 months. Seventy-five percent of score variance was attributable to patient differences and 25% to timepoint, yielding phi = 0.93 (excellent consistency). We aimed to validate the Patient Dignity Inventory (PDI) among ARF survivors by evaluating internal consistency and generalizability and establishing a minimal clinically important difference (MCID). Prospective cohort study (June 2022 to April 2023) of mechanically ventilated (≥24 h) adults in four University of Pennsylvania hospitals' ICUs.
articles.analysis: Use as background evidence; look for confounding and whether the exposure is modifiable at the bedside.

pico.population: adults in four University of Pennsylvania hospitals' ICUs
pico.intervention: Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective.
pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: Patients completed the PDI within 1 month of ICU discharge and at 3, 6, and 12 months. We assessed internal consistency using Cronbach's alpha (reliability of questions measuring the same construct; range 0-1) and exploratory factor analysis to identify dignity-related distress domains. We evaluated generalizability using multilevel mixed-effects modeling...

keyResults: We calculated MCID by averaging anchor-based (EuroQol-5 Dimension MCID = 0.11 from baseline to 12 months) and distribution-based (average of se of the mean, 0.3 × sd, and 5% of baseline score range) estimates.Of 967 eligible patients, 103 patients (11%) enrolled at baseline, with 70% retention at 12 months.
Seventy-five percent of score variance was attributable to patient differences and 25% to timepoint, yielding phi = 0.93 (excellent consistency).
Patients completed the PDI within 1 month of ICU discharge and at 3, 6, and 12 months.

We assessed internal consistency using Cronbach's alpha (reliability of questions measuring the same construct; range 0-1) and exploratory factor analysis to identify dignity-related distress domains.
criticalAppraisal: Observational evidence: useful for association and prognosis, but residual confounding is likely.
Check confounding by indication, adjustment variables, immortal-time bias, and missing outcome data.
articles.practiceImpact: Use as background evidence; look for confounding and whether the exposure is modifiable at the bedside.
